input.prompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI issue where the keyboard covers the bottom text input on Android. They mention that `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` no longer works in .NET 10. Let me provide guidance based on what I know about this change.

In .NET MAUI / Android, the `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` mode was deprecated in Android 11 (API 30) and has been increasingly unreliable. In .NET 10 (which targets newer Android APIs), the recommended approach shifted to using **Edge-to-Edge** display and **WindowInsets** handling, or using `AdjustPan` combined with proper layout techniques.


The core issue is that Android deprecated `SOFT_INPUT_ADJUST_RESIZE` in API 30, so in .NET 9+ MAUI it's no longer viable. The better approaches now are using `AdjustPan` with proper scrolling containers, or handling window insets directly to adjust your layout when the keyboard appears. In .NET 10, MAUI's Edge-to-Edge default changes how soft input mode behaves, so the modern approach is to handle `WindowInsets` directly and adjust padding based on keyboard height rather than relying solely on the Android manifest attribute.

I'm setting up a platform-specific handler that configures the soft input mode to resize the window when the keyboard appears, which lets you detect those inset changes and reposition your chat content accordingly.

Actually, let me step back—MAUI has a built-in `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` feature that handles this automatically in .NET 8 and later, so for most chat apps that's the simplest solution to try first before diving into custom inset handling.
